value.value: A distinct form of pasta, characterized by its geometry, size, and regional origin.
An Italian region associated with a pasta shape's origin.
A sauce traditionally paired with a pasta shape.
The geometric form category of a pasta shape (e.g. tubular, ribbon, strand).
A size classification for pasta shapes.
The surface finish of a pasta shape, affecting sauce adhesion.
Tube-shaped pasta cut diagonally at the ends, from the Italian word for 'quill'.
Bow-tie or butterfly shaped pasta. From 'farfalla' (butterfly).
Ring-shaped stuffed pasta, traditionally filled with meat. From 'tortello' (small cake).
Wide, flat sheets of pasta, layered with sauce and cheese.
Rice-shaped small pasta, used in soups and salads. From 'orzo' (barley).
Very broad, flat ribbon pasta. From 'pappare' (to gobble up).
Short, thin, twisted pasta from Liguria. Traditionally paired with pesto.
Thick spaghetti-like pasta with a hole through the center. From 'buco' (hole).
Shell-shaped pasta that traps sauce inside. From 'conchiglia' (shell).
Small ridged Sardinian gnocchi-like pasta, also called 'gnocchetti sardi'.
Sauces built primarily on cheese, often with egg or pepper.
Corkscrew-shaped pasta from Trapani, Sicily. Traditionally made by wrapping dough around a knitting needle ('buso').
Flat ribbon pasta, approximately 6mm wide. Egg-based, from Roman cuisine. From 'fettuccia' (ribbon). Narrower than tagliatelle.
Flat ribbon pasta, approximately 8mm wide. Egg-based, from Bolognese cuisine. From 'tagliare' (to cut). Wider than fettuccine.
A slow-cooked meat sauce, varying by region. The base of many Italian pasta dishes.
Ragù alla bolognese: a meat-based sauce from Bologna using minced beef/pork, soffritto, tomato paste, and wine.
Roman sauce of guanciale, egg yolk, Pecorino Romano, and black pepper. Never contains cream.
Sauce from Amatrice (Lazio): guanciale, Pecorino Romano, tomato, and chili. Related to gricia.
Spicy tomato sauce with garlic and red chili peppers. 'Arrabbiata' means 'angry'.
Ancient Roman sauce of Pecorino Romano and black pepper, emulsified with pasta water. One of Rome's four classic pastas.
Sauces with tomato as the primary base ingredient.
Sauces based on cream or béchamel.
Sauces built on olive oil as the primary fat, often with garlic (aglio e olio).
Uncooked sauces made by crushing herbs, nuts, cheese, and oil. Includes Genovese (basil, pine nut) and Trapanese (tomato, almond) variants.
Pasta served in brodo (in broth), typically a clear meat or vegetable stock.
Sauces based on melted butter, often with sage (burro e salvia) or Parmigiano.
Sauces where meat is the primary ingredient, including ragù and other slow-cooked preparations.
Pasta enclosing a filling. Classified as a geometry category for modeling purposes, though technically a construction method rather than a pure geometric form.
Hollow cylindrical pasta, cut straight or diagonally. Sauce flows through and clings to the interior.
Flat, wide pasta cut into strips. Width varies from narrow (linguine) to very broad (pappardelle).
Long, thin, solid cylindrical pasta. Diameter distinguishes varieties (capellini < spaghetti < spaghettoni).
Broad, flat pasta sheets used for layering or wrapping.
Pasta formed into distinctive three-dimensional shapes (shells, ears, bows, grains) that don't fit other geometric categories.
Pasta twisted or coiled into a helix or corkscrew form. Traps chunky sauces in its grooves.
Relates a pasta shape to its geometric form category.
Relates a pasta shape to its size classification.
Relates a pasta shape to its surface texture, which affects sauce adhesion.
Relates a pasta shape to the Italian region where it traditionally originated.
Relates a pasta shape to a sauce it is traditionally served with.
The primary Italian name of a pasta shape.
The etymological origin of a pasta shape's name.
Pasta small enough for soups or broth (pastina). Typically under 2cm.
Standard-sized pasta for plated dishes, typically 3-8cm.
Large pasta shapes, typically over 8cm or used for baking/layering.
Smooth surface finish, produced by Teflon extrusion dies. Less sauce adhesion than rigata.
Ridged surface finish, produced by bronze extrusion dies. Increases sauce adhesion.
Southern Italian region centered on Naples. Birthplace of dried pasta traditions.
Northwestern coastal region centered on Genoa. Known for pesto and olive oil-based cuisine.
Northern region spanning Bologna to Parma. Heartland of fresh egg pasta and rich meat sauces.
Central Italian region centered on Rome. Home to the four classic Roman pastas.
Italy's largest island. Pasta traditions influenced by Arab, Greek, and Norman cultures.
Southeastern region forming the 'heel' of Italy. Known for durum wheat pasta and vegetable pairings.
Mediterranean island with distinct pastoral cuisine. Unique pasta shapes often use saffron.
Central Italian region centered on Florence. Known for rustic, broad pasta with game sauces.
